articles.title: Blocked by censors, China's animal lovers take fight against abuse offline and overseas
articles.summary: The killing of a dog and her puppies in Guangdong has sparked outrage inside and outside the country.
articles.full_text:
Image source, Supplied Image caption, 
From Taiwan to the US, activists are lobbying for China to pass laws against animal cruelty By Gemini Cheng ,  BBC News Chinese ,  Reporting from Hong Kong   and   Kelly Ng ,  Reporting from Singapore 
- Published 22 July 2026 Updated 23 July 2026 
When videos showing the horrific killing of a stray dog and her puppies sparked outrage on the Chinese internet in June, Beijing's censorship machine kicked into action. 
But what was unusual this time was that it pitted the shocked and angry voices against authorities who said they were concerned about the four boys involved, all of them younger than 14. 
China's Communist Party regime tightly controls media and the internet to suppress criticism of the state and to manage public anger. But in this case authorities cited the age of the boys as they urged people to withhold their comments and scrubbed social media posts about the incident. 
"We urge the public not to disseminate related information, to reject cyberbullying, and to work together to create a good environment for the growth of minors," reads an official statement. 
But the restrictions only seemed to spur the outcry. China's animal lovers have now taken their fight offline and abroad, even putting up billboards in New York to drum up support. 
In China, posts about the incident are still drawing thousands of comments across social media, three weeks after the incident. There is shock, anger and sorrow, as well as demands for laws against animal cruelty, which China currently does not have. 
Disturbing footage showing the boys in the city of Jieyang, Guangdong province, hitting three puppies with sticks before setting their mother on fire emerged online at the end of June. The boys were seen laughing as the dog, named Wang Wang, burned. 
Jieyang authorities said last month that the boys would not face criminal charges and would instead be sent to a "specialised school", but did not elaborate. 
Some reports suggest the boys filmed themselves and uploaded the video online, but police have not commented on that. Censors have tried to block the video to protect the boys' identity, although some people argue that it should stay. 
Their identities have not been revealed, but snippets of the video with their faces visible continue to circulate online. There are also unverified posts claiming to be close-ups of the boys' faces, raising the risk of other children being wrongly identified. 
Image source, Getty Images Image caption, 
Animal cruelty is not punishable by law in China 
Countries around the world restrict information about child offenders or set a high bar for identifying them. 
For instance, media coverage and public access to juvenile court documents are often restricted to minimise psychological harm caused by undue publicity. 
In this case, Chinese authorities have not completely banned the discussion yet. Keywords such as "Wang Wang" and "animal protection" came up as trending search terms on Monday, but posts related to them started to disappear towards the end of this week. Some users have also reported that their ability to share others' posts has been restricted. 
It is hard to say if the deleted or restricted content had named or identified any of the four boys. Officials have not said anything more on the matter. 
Posts by several celebrities, including Chinese actor Du Jiang and Taiwanese actress Joe Chen, who had called for China to strengthen protection for animals also disappeared. But it's unclear if these were taken down by the celebrities themselves. 
Chinese users have refused to back down though, with some turning to advocates abroad to air Wang Wang's plight. 
It is hard to say why this, of all the sensitive issues that are censored in China, has galvanised such pushback. 
Part of the reason could be that it's the latest in a string of animal abuse cases that have made headlines recently. And the footage seems to have had a visceral impact. Animal lover PL tells BBC Chinese she found it "affecting in a particularly severe way". 
"We can't make our voices heard, so many people [outside China] are actively helping us to share this message," the woman in eastern China says. 
The International Organisation for Animal Protection based in Italy says it has received more than 100 reports from people across the world, calling for the organisation to intervene. It added that it had sought more information from Chinese authorities. 
Image source, Supplied Image caption, 
China's animal lovers have taken their fight global, with some putting up billboards in places as far away as New York 
In New York City's Times Square, one of the busiest pedestrian intersections in the world, photographs of Wang Wang come up on a gigantic digital screen every 35 minutes. 
One of the captions reads: "She was loved. She wanted to live. She should still be here." Similar billboards have been put up on a building near Taipei 101, an iconic 101-floor skyscraper in Taiwan. This one includes a picture of Wang Wang nursing her puppies. "This planet is their home too. Justice for Wang Wang," the caption reads. 
CK, a Taiwanese woman who spent NT$30,000 ($930; £692) putting up the billboard, says she hopes it can foster "a sense of unity". 
"It makes everyone feel like we're speaking out together on this issue, rather than airing our individual opinions online," she tells BBC Chinese. CK has also set up accounts on platforms like Reddit and X to share the animals' plight. 
"The world need to know about the cruelty these dogs have been subject to," CK says, adding that she couldn't sleep or eat properly for days after seeing a clip of the brutal killing. 
Elara, another Taiwanese woman, used AI to translate a summary of the incident into more than 10 languages, such as Japanese, Portuguese and Russian, which have been circulated online. 
Residents in Hong Kong and Macau have raised funds to put up posters on buses and trains. 
Within mainland China, the debate continues, with much of the conversation centred around creating awareness to demand laws against animal cruelty. 
A striking cartoon of Wang Wang and her puppies caught the eye of many football fans during a domestic game at a stadium in Dalian on Saturday. "Every life deserves respect and kindness," read the cartoon that was displayed on a huge screen before the game. 
Homegrown businesses, such as beauty brand Amortals and streetwear label Wah Yan Ching Nin, have also placed ads outside shopping malls across the country, calling for an end to animal abuse. 
Stickers and car decals featuring Wang Wang and her puppies have also been put up for sale on China's largest e-commerce platform Taobao. 
Some of these efforts have met roadblocks. Wan Jia Li, a mall in the city of Changsha, was reportedly instructed to remove billboards featuring Wang Wang. 
"We can't do animal-protection billboards anymore... My boss told me so. I'm not sure if the notice came from the government, or why we have been asked to take them down," an employee in the mall's management team told the BBC. 
Still, people have continued to rally online. 
"Let's keep posting. Don't let the heat of discussion cool down," reads a comment on Weibo. 
"Constantly speaking up does not mean disturbing social order. [We're doing this] to let more people see it and realise it's such an important thing that we do not mistreat animals," reads another. 
Related topics
- China 
- Asia 
- Taiwan 
- Hong Kong

articles.title: China's Moonshot AI stole from Anthropic, Trump tech adviser says
articles.summary: The allegations come as Chinese AI companies are facing increased US government scrutiny.
articles.full_text:
Image source, Future Publishing via Getty Images Image caption, 
Kimi K3 is an AI model developed by Beijing-based Moonshot AI By Osmond Chia Business reporter 
- Published 23 July 2026 Updated 5 hours ago 
A White House adviser has accused China's Moonshot AI of a "large scale" effort to steal the capabilities of top US artificial intelligence (AI) models. 
US President Donald Trump's Science and Technology adviser Michael Kratsios said Moonshot AI carried out the campaign through what is known as distillation - when a weaker AI model extracts answers from a stronger one. 
Moonshot also gained access to restricted cutting-edge Nvidia servers to train its models, Kratsios said in a social post , external on Wednesday. 
The BBC has contacted Moonshot, Anthropic, the White House, and Nvidia for comment. 
Kratsios said on X that the US government has information that Moonshot AI "distilled" capabilities from Anthropic's Fable AI for the development of its K3 model. 
Kimi K3 gained attention around the world after it was unveiled last week , with many believing it to have narrowed the gap between Western and Chinese AI models. Moonshot said its K3 model is able to rival top US technology. 
Moonshot is likely to have also used servers powered by Nvidia's GB300 Grace Blackwell computing platform, Kratsios said. 
Washington restricted the export of Nvidia's most advanced chips in 2022 over concerns that they could be used by the Chinese military. 
Since then, government's around the world have cracked down on the smuggling of the chips. 
In response to queries from the BBC, the Chinese embassy in Washington referred to remarks made by foreign ministry spokesperson Lin Jian on Wednesday, saying China opposes the "politicising" of trade and tech issues. 
Lin said China's AI development is centred on "joint contribution for shared benefit", urging all countries to take a "people-centred approach". 
Kratsios' allegations come just a day after Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Tuesday the US would examine whether Chinese AI models have stolen the capabilities from American rivals. 
On Wednesday, Bessent also said that sanctions "will be on the table" when Chinese companies "cross the line" into intellectual property (IP) theft by conducting "industrial-scale distillation attacks". 
"We support open-source AI and the innovation it unlocks. But open source is not open season on American IP," Bessent said on social media. 
The Chinese government has not yet issued a public statement in response to the latest allegations but has previously said that its country's development of AI was "the result of its own dedication and effort as well as international cooperation". 
The increased scrutiny by the US of Chinese AI companies also comes as Trump is expected to meet his Chinese counterpart Xi Jinping in September. 
Moonshot AI will release Kimi K3 as an open-source model on 27 July, making it the first of its scale that can be freely downloaded and customised by developers and members of the public. 
In June, Anthropic accused Chinese e-commerce and tech giant Alibaba of "illicitly" extracting the capabilities of its Claude AI models using distillation attacks. 
Anthropic urged Congress to penalise the companies behind the alleged attacks and heighten measures to prevent US technology from being stolen. 
The White House said in April that it would work more closely with US AI companies to combat "industrial-scale campaigns" by foreign rivals to steal technology. 
Kratsios said in a memo at the time that the campaigns were aimed to "systematically undermine American research and development and access proprietary information". 
Related topics
- International Business 
- Artificial intelligence 
- China 
- White House 
More on this story
- 
OpenAI says its AI went rogue and launched 'unprecedented' cyber-attack 
- Published 1 day ago 
- 
China's Moonshot AI claims Kimi K3 can rival OpenAI and Anthropic 
- Published 6 days ago

articles.title: British woman jailed for blackmail after accusing banker of rape in Hong Kong
articles.summary: Isabel Rose was convicted of trying to extort £100,000 from the UK banker and perverting the course of justice.
articles.full_text:
By Jessica Rawnsley 
- Published 22 July 2026 
A British woman has been jailed for six years in Hong Kong for blackmail and perverting the course of justice after a court found she made a false allegation of rape. 
Isabel Rose, 26, from Hackney in east London, alleged that a British banker raped her after she travelled to Hong Kong to visit him in January 2024. 
The man was arrested after she reported the incident but later released without charge. Rose was subsequently arrested and charged. 
Local media reported that Rose was in Hong Kong's District Court on Wednesday for sentencing after being convicted in March. She had denied the charges. 
'I didn't wanna have sex' 
According to local press reports, Judge Adriana Tse sentenced her to five years in prison for blackmail and three years for perverting the course of justice, with one year to be served consecutively. 
Prosecutors argued that Rose fabricated the allegation in an attempt to extort £100,000 ($134,000) from the man, whose identity was withheld. Rose denied the charges throughout the trial and has consistently maintained that she was raped. 
Rose had travelled to Hong Kong on 31 January 2024 after meeting the man while in Thailand a month earlier, according to court documents. 
At the centre of the prosecution case were WhatsApp messages exchanged between the pair in the hours and days after the alleged rape on 1 February at his apartment. 
In one message sent the following morning, Rose wrote: "Are you annoyed I didn't want to have sex," to which the man replied: "What no of course not. Was impressed by your restraint." 
Later that day, Rose wrote: "You violated me last night, I didn't wanna have sex." 
He replied: "I'm really deeply sorry, I was slightly drunk and I misread your signals, it's no excuse and I know it doesn't make it any better." 
In a later exchange, Rose wrote: "You basically raped me." The man replied: "That's not what I'm into at all and I feel sick." 
The pair later exchanged messages about money. According to the written judgement of the verdict published in March, the man agreed to reimburse Rose for her travel and accommodation. 
In one message, Rose wrote: "Send me the right amount you owe me for everything. Think about that. Think carefully." 
He transferred £5,000 that day and tried to send a further £5,000. 
Rose subsequently wrote: "You've given 10% of what I'm owed. 100% of my soul gone." 
In a later message, the man wrote: "But I don't have 100k, I have no way of paying you that." 
Rose reported the rape to police on 3 February. The man was arrested and questioned before being released without charge. Rose was arrested the following day and later charged with blackmail and perverting the course of public justice. 
'Cruel and evil' 
In her verdict, Judge AN Tse Ching relied in part on the messages exchanged after the encounter, according to the South China Morning Post , external . 
At Wednesday's sentencing, Tse said the defendant had committed a "cruel and evil" offence by extorting money from the man, the newspaper reported. 
"The defendant took advantage of [the man]'s kindness, naivety, friendship and romantic interest," Judge Tse said. 
She called Rose "an author of her own wrongdoing" and said her false report to police had been "vengeful and wicked". 
Related topics
- Hong Kong 
- London 
- Hackney 
- China 
- Asia

articles.title: Democratic lawmakers propose bank funded by China tariffs to boost US manufacturing
articles.summary: Exclusive: Ro Khanna says ‘reindustrialization’ effort would provide grants, loans and investments to smaller businessesDemocrats in the US Congress have laid out plans for what they say would be the “boldest” bid to revitalize the country’s industrial heartlands since the second world war – using revenue raised by tariffs on China.A bill introduced this week would create a new bank to strengthen domestic manufacturing, provided with up to $15bn in funds each year. Continue reading...
articles.full_text:
The Democratic lawmakers (from left) Tom Suozzi, Ro Khanna and Debbie Dingell have proposed a new bank. Photograph: Getty Images 
View image in fullscreen 
The Democratic lawmakers (from left) Tom Suozzi, Ro Khanna and Debbie Dingell have proposed a new bank. Photograph: Getty Images 
Democratic lawmakers propose bank funded by China tariffs to boost US manufacturing
Exclusive: Ro Khanna says ‘reindustrialization’ effort would provide grants, loans and investments to smaller businesses 
Democrats in the US Congress have laid out plans for what they say would be the “boldest” bid to revitalize the country’s industrial heartlands since the second world war – using revenue raised by tariffs on China. 
A bill introduced this week would create a new bank to strengthen domestic manufacturing, provided with up to $15bn in funds each year. 
The bill, led by US representatives Ro Khanna, Tom Suozzi and Debbie Dingell, is entitled the Industrial Bank for American Manufacturing Act. 
“This is one of the boldest industrialization proposals since FDR’s industrialization efforts , and it’s in the tradition of Hamilton’s efforts ,” Khanna claimed in an interview. “This bank would support small and medium-sized manufacturers who are making things that we’re currently importing, and it would help thousands of our manufacturers across America to make things here, instead of importing them.” 
It would create a new fund to provide grants, loans and equity investments using existing section 301 tariff revenue, directing up to 50% of collected revenue from tariffs on imports from the People’s Republic of China , capped at $15bn annually. 
Those funds would be directed to rebuild manufacturing in the US, rather than deposited into the US treasury’s general fund. 
Khanna said the funds would target de-industrialized areas of the US that have been hit hard by the decline of US manufacturing, such as Johnstown, Pennsylvania, a once-booming center of the US steel industry ; Lordstown, Ohio, where General Motors shut down a plant in 2019; the Downriver region of Michigan in the Detroit area, where the decline of the auto industry has hit the region particularly hard; and the Lower Bucks county’s Delaware River corridor in Pennsylvania, where Khanna grew up. 
“It would be an effort to re-industrialize the country,” added Khanna. “We saw this, of course, with FDR during world war two with the war reconstruction board, and we saw it with Hamilton. We need what I call the ‘modern Marshall plan for America’. This is part of my vision for a Marshall plan for America, that this is the most patriotic and boldest jobs agenda for industrialization since FDR and Bill Knudsen in the 1940s.” 
The bill caps loans at $500m, and requires congressional approval for loans over $100m from the fund. 
Americans have paid some form of tariffs on imports from China for decades, with Donald Trump enacting large tariffs on China in 2018, which were maintained under Joe Biden, and then increased again by Trump during his second presidential term. 
Manufacturing in the US has long been in decline , peaking in 1979 at about 19.6m jobs, before falling sharply, to about 12.6m in 2026. 
This decline has continued despite claims and promises from the Trump administration to revive the industry. According to data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics, US employment in manufacturing has declined by 75,000 jobs since January 2025. 
The legislation follows a “ heartland tour ” Khanna recently took across the US midwest about the effects Trump’s tariffs are having on workers, manufacturers and farmers. 
On the tour, Khanna recounted hearing from a small manufacturer outside of Cleveland, Ohio, who needed capital to be able to make a part in the US, but was relying on imports from Asia in lieu of it. 
“They could make the part here, but they didn’t have the money. They needed about a million dollars of capital to do it,” he said. “We have a country right now where all the capital is going to build AI, technology apps and financial firms, but we need capital also for our small and medium-sized businesses, for our manufacturers. 
“We need to be developing not just New York and Silicon Valley and Austin – we need to be developing industry in the midwest, in the south-west, in the south, and I believe an industrial investment bank is a concrete step towards that.” 
- 
- 
- 
- Explore more on these topics

- Manufacturing sector 
- Banking 
- US economy 
- Tariffs 
- China 
- Donald Trump 
- news Share

articles.title: ‘A statement of freedom’: T-shirt slogan goes viral and book sales rocket in Taiwan after Hong Kong raids
articles.summary: Taiwanese people look on with concern at fate of Hong Kong’s independent booksellers, after series of arrests in recent monthsWalking out of one of Hong Kong’s last remaining independent bookstores, hands behind her back but head held high, the woman’s T-shirt was emblazoned with a simple slogan: “I am a bookstore employee”.Escorted by police, the woman was among five people arrested during raids on two bookstores last week, suspected of displaying and selling publications with “seditious” content, a national security crime which carries a maximum sentence of 10 years. Continue reading...
articles.full_text:
An independent bookstore employee is escorted away from 'Have a Nice Stay' independent bookstore by Hong Kong national security police. Photograph: David Chan/SOPA Images/Shutterstock 
View image in fullscreen 
An independent bookstore employee is escorted away from 'Have a Nice Stay' independent bookstore by Hong Kong national security police. Photograph: David Chan/SOPA Images/Shutterstock 
‘A statement of freedom’: T-shirt slogan goes viral and book sales rocket in Taiwan after Hong Kong raids
Taiwanese people look on with concern at fate of Hong Kong’s independent booksellers, after series of arrests in recent months 
W alking out of one of Hong Kong’s last remaining independent bookstores, hands behind her back but head held high, the woman’s T-shirt was emblazoned with a simple slogan : “I am a bookstore employee”. 
Escorted by police, the woman was among five people arrested during raids on two bookstores last week , suspected of displaying and selling publications with “seditious” content, a national security crime which carries a maximum sentence of 10 years. 
Since then, the T-shirt phrase has spread far and wide on Taiwanese social media amid concern for the fate of Hong Kong’s booksellers. 
Taiwan is now the only place in the Chinese-speaking world where people can openly express their feelings about repression by the Chinese government. 
On the social media platform Threads, booksellers, lawmakers and activists shared the hashtag “I am a bookstore employee” to show their support for Hong Kong. Taiwan’s president, Lai Ching-te, also weighed in, writing on Facebook: “Ideas and words should never be silenced by political pressure”. 
It is the third time in recent months that people in Hong Kong linked to independent bookstores have been arrested. 
The Hong Kong authorities have not specified what the allegedly seditious books were in the latest raids. But after the arrest, Hong Kong’s security chief, Chris Tang, said that booksellers had a responsibility to ensure their books were lawful, “just like merchants selling food have to make sure their food won’t make people sick”. All five arrested in last week’s raids have since been released on bail. Five arrested in Hong Kong bookstore raids in ‘seditious’ materials crackdown 
Read more 
The Hong Kong police declined to comment further, but reports that the offending books may have included Let Only Red Flowers Bloom: Identity and Belonging in Xi Jinping’s China , written by NPR journalist Emily Feng, prompted a run on the book in Taiwan. 
The Taiwanese publisher, Acropolis Publishing, ordered an emergency reprint of 10,000 additional copies last week. 
Sara Sung, a senior editor at Acropolis, said some readers were buying copies as “a statement of democracy and freedom”. “They’re showing that they will not be silenced or intimidated by censorship from China,” Sung said. 
Chang Hui-ju, the owner of Touat Books, an independent bookshop in Taipei, Taiwan’s capital, said she was completely out of stock of Feng’s book. “Before this, hardly anyone paid attention to it,” Chang said. “But right after the incident, the book sold out everywhere.” 
View image in fullscreen 
A man browses the shelves inside Kuo’s Astral Bookshop, in Taiwan. Photograph: Yu-chen Li 
The ban ‘only made me more curious’
Another Taipei store, Kuo’s Astral Bookshop, also sold out of the book. One customer looking in vain for a copy, Wu Jia-xuan, said she was not normally interested in China-related books but that her interest had been piqued by the Hong Kong arrests. “The fact that it had been banned only made [me] more curious,” Wu said. 
Feng’s book examines Chinese leader Xi Jinping’s ideological control over the country. One chapter, titled The Bookseller, focuses on the prominent Hong Kong bookseller Lam Wing-kee . 
Lam was arrested by Chinese authorities in 2015 but later escaped custody and fled to Taiwan, where he re-opened his bookstore, Causeway Bay Books, in Taipei. Lam died earlier this month and the fate of his Taipei store is uncertain: the closed-up shopfront is covered with notes, flowers and cans of beer left by his supporters. 
View image in fullscreen 
A poster of the late Lam Wing-kee and notes left by supporters are taped to the front of Causeway Bay Books. Photograph: Yu-chen Li 
Lam’s story charts the uneasy and complex relationship that Taiwan and Hong Kong have with each other, as well as with mainland China. 
Taiwan has never been part of the People’s Republic of China. But Beijing has long claimed the self-governing island as part of its territory and has vowed to unify it with China. One path to peaceful unification, suggested by Beijing , is for Taiwan to be ruled under a “one country, two systems” framework, similar to Hong Kong. Over 80% of Taiwanese reject that idea. 
View image in fullscreen 
A sign in Touat Books, an independent bookshop in Taipei, reads ‘Great books China won’t publish’. Photograph: Yu-chen Li 
In Taiwan, politicians from the ruling Democratic Progressive party (DPP), which rejects the idea of closer ties with China, have pointed to Hong Kong as an example of the dangers of what happens under Chinese rule. Beijing’s crackdown on Hong Kong in 2019 has been credited with galvanising DPP voters to win the election for Tsai Ing-wen, Lai’s DPP predecessor, in 2020. 
On Monday, discussing the Hong Kong raids, DPP legislator Wu Pei-yi accused China of “burning books”. 
But in Taiwan, rhetorical support for Hong Kong has rarely been matched by practical policies, such as taking in refugees. For DPP politicians, “it’s very easy to use Hong Kong as a way to frame what will happen to Taiwan if the People’s Republic of China is able to ‘unify’ Taiwan,” said Lev Nachman, a professor of political science at National Taiwan University in Taipei. But actual support has been “shallow”, Nachman said. 
For Chinese people, Taiwan is now the last place in the Chinese-speaking world where they can freely access books without the threat of Communist party censorship. 
One student, originally from Fujian province, visited Touat Books on Sunday looking for a copy of Feng’s book. She’d read about the Hong Kong raids on social media, news which kept her awake until 2am. “In mainland China, none of this would come up on your feed … It was quite a shock,” she said. “This shouldn’t be happening. I think independent thought should still be allowed – at least in Hong Kong.” 
- 
- 
-

articles.title: The Guardian view on Chinese workers picking up the pen: poetry in motion | Editorial
articles.summary: A food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourersThe hundreds of millions who have built modern China from the ground up – sweeping its streets, labouring in its factories and ferrying its goods – are often overlooked. But they refuse to go unheard. Last week, a food delivery rider won China’s top literary award. Wang Jibing, 56, first caught attention when a poem about his experiences went viral in 2022. The following year, Hu Anyan’s memoir, I Deliver Parcels in Beijing, became a bestseller.In the early 20th century, Chinese intellectuals sought to capture the harsh realities of working-class lives. But it was under Mao Zedong that the idea of writing by as well as about the masses became reality, with labourers encouraged to take up the pen. In an era when the party tried to govern the language even of diaries and personal letters, the parameters of style and content were strict, and this enthusiasm swiftly diminished after Mao. But workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please <a href="mailto:guardian.letters@theguardian.com?body=Please%20include%20your%20name,%20full%20postal%20address%20and%20phone%20number%20with%20your%20letter%20below.%20Let
articles.full_text:
‘Workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.’ Photograph: Yuan He/AP 
View image in fullscreen 
‘Workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above.’ Photograph: Yuan He/AP 
The Guardian view on Chinese workers picking up the pen: poetry in motion
Editorial 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
- 
A food delivery rider has won the country’s highest literary award. It is part of a growing trend for writing by labourers 
T he hundreds of millions who have built modern China from the ground up – sweeping its streets, labouring in its factories and ferrying its goods – are often overlooked. But they refuse to go unheard. Last week, a food delivery rider won China’s top literary award . Wang Jibing, 56, first caught attention when a poem about his experiences went viral in 2022. The following year, Hu Anyan’s memoir , I Deliver Parcels in Beijing, became a bestseller. 
In the early 20th century, Chinese intellectuals sought to capture the harsh realities of working-class lives. But it was under Mao Zedong that the idea of writing by as well as about the masses became reality, with labourers encouraged to take up the pen. In an era when the party tried to govern the language even of diaries and personal letters, the parameters of style and content were strict, and this enthusiasm swiftly diminished after Mao. But workers’ writing has seen a revival in the 21st century. This time, it is bottom-up rather than directed from above. 
Maoist literature was meant to educate and mobilise the masses. Heavy labour was portrayed as offering a kind of transcendence , with workers becoming almost superhuman in their efforts. Contrast that with the testimony of the poet Xiao Hai, whose memoir, Adrift in the South, was published in English in May. Forced by family poverty to work on assembly lines from the age of 15, he writes of his “bitterness and regret … when I realise I let the best decade of my life slip away from me on the factory floor”. 
The individual scrabble to get by has replaced paeans to collective toil, and the sense of hope has given way to numbness or despair. The image of the screw once represented workers’ pride in their small contribution to a greater cause. But in the poetry of Xu Lizhi , a Foxconn worker who killed himself in 2014, it reflects their insignificance and replaceability. 
These accounts of exploitation and dangers, of bodies and souls ground down, may seem unsparing. But the Communist party has little tolerance for work that touches on topics such as the role of corrupt officials or collective organisation. Many grassroots writers post online, where their work can easily be deleted. The well‑known poet Zheng Xiaoqiong collected the stories of fellow labourers in Woman Worker, but it has never been issued in full because publishers demanded extensive changes. 
The work of this new generation has drawn wider attention in the last few years. Fan Yusu’s essay on life as a migrant worker made her a literary sensation in 2017. In the pandemic , some began to notice the low-paid workers who were keeping the country running. A growing sense of precarity among the middle classes may have contributed. Labourers have been at the sharpest end of China’s breakneck development, but others share their concerns about the country’s path – and fear what happens if their status slips. 
These narratives resonate with readers outside China too. They delineate the unsavoury underpinnings of the globalised economy and speak to the unease that many feel with it. I Deliver Parcels in Beijing has been sold to 16 countries; an English edition came out last year. In many places, there are still more books about workers than by them. Where is the English-language bestseller by an Amazon warehouse operative, or the viral poetry about care work on a zero-hours contract? 
- 
Do you have an opinion on the issues raised in this article? If you would like to submit a response of up to 300 words by email to be considered for publication in our letters section, please click here .

articles.title: Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal
articles.summary: Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missileGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastPenny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week.The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict. Continue reading...
articles.full_text:
Australian foreign minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart on Tuesday night on the sidelines of the Asean summit. Photograph: Lukas Coch/AAP 
View image in fullscreen 
Australian foreign minister Penny Wong met with her Chinese counterpart on Tuesday night on the sidelines of the Asean summit. Photograph: Lukas Coch/AAP 
Australia to warn China it won’t be bullied by ‘provocative‘ actions as it builds military and nuclear arsenal 
Foreign minister Penny Wong will deliver speech criticising China’s recent launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile 
- 
Get our breaking news email , free app or daily news podcast 
Penny Wong will warn China that Australia and other neighbouring nations will not be bullied by its “provocative” actions, after its involvement in another “destabilising and dangerous” incident this week. 
The foreign affairs minister – who will deliver the address on Thursday in the Philippines, where the 59th Asean foreign minister’s meeting is being held – will also caution world leaders against placing a toll on the strait of Hormuz after both Iran and the US threatened to do amid the protracted conflict. 
The speech comes as the crisis in the Middle East escalates once again, driving up fuel prices by 40 cents in three weeks amid dwindling global oil reserves. 
Off the back of China’s launch of a nuclear-capable submarine-launched missile in the South China Sea earlier in July, Wong will say the test “came with too little information and notification”. 
“It comes in the context of China’s rapid military buildup, including of its nuclear arsenal, without the transparency or reassurance the region expects,” the foreign minister will say. 
“Which is why Australia has described the test as provocative and destabilising.” 
Wong met with China’s foreign minister, Wang Yi, on the sidelines of Asean on Tuesday night AEST in their 12th meeting. 
Wong had earlier criticised China’s conduct as “destabilising and dangerous” after the Philippine military accused the China Coast Guard of clubbing a Filipino sailor with a baton on Monday on a disputed shoal in the South China Sea. RBA now twice as likely to hike interest rates as US-Iran war drives fuel prices higher 
Read more 
“We will continue to register our concerns about China’s vessels engaging in destabilising and dangerous conduct in the South China Sea, including towards Philippine, Vietnamese, Malaysian, and Indonesian vessels,” Wong will say on Thursday. 
China’s long-range missile test in the South Pacific happened just hours after Australia signed a defence agreement with Fiji, inciting widespread condemnation . The prime minister, Anthony Albanese, expressed concern at the missile’s long-range capabilities and the possibility to cause “considerable, considerable damage were it to be weaponised”. 
In Manila, Wong will also warn the region will suffer if the strait of Hormuz is “weaponised” and the US and Iran fail to maintain a ceasefire. 
Heavy strikes have resumed between the warring nations with the US launching consecutive rounds on Iran in an attempt to force the strait’s reopening. 
Iran’s president on Tuesday said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen have also announced a blockade of Saudi ports. 
The renewed outbreak of fighting has sent the international Brent crude benchmark surging by 23% over the past two weeks and back within reach of $US90 a barrel. skip past newsletter promotion 
after newsletter promotion 
The surge in cost has pushed diesel above 220 cents per litre and petrol above 187 cents in some Australian cities. Luke Yeaman, the Commonwealth Bank chief economist, has predicted the federal government may have to restore the fuel excise discount back to 32 cents. 
Wong will tell the audience much of the region was fuel- and energy-dependent on the strait of Hormuz, presenting a “grave challenge”. 
“Across south-east Asia, energy prices have spiked, costs for families and businesses increased, and we continue to see ongoing pressure on food production,” Wong will say. 
“The strait of Hormuz shows what we in this region have always understood. That people suffer when a strait is weaponised. And we all know that the costs of tolls on any chokepoint would not be solely economic. 
“Our people depend on straits and sea lanes remaining open.” 
Wong will tell Asean nations their assistance in plugging Australia’s supply and trade gaps during the conflict is for the region’s benefit. 
“Because what goes to Australia as fuel and fertiliser comes back to the region as food,” she will say. 
With additional reporting by Patrick Commins
